title: A post-Newtonian Gravitational Collapse Model from Linearized Gravity
authors: Rudi B. P. Pietsch, Luciano Petruzziello, Martin B. Plenio
abstract: We introduce a general gravity-related collapse mechanism based on linearized gravity. Starting from the weak-field limit of general relativity, gravitoelectromagnetism suggests an effective coupling between the gravitoelectric potential and the mass density distribution. At the same time, it provides a similar relation for the gravitomagnetic vector potential and the mass current. Following a hybrid (classical-quantum) dynamics approach, these couplings lead to a master equation whose non-unitary part is determined by the underlying mass distribution and currents. When the gravitoelectric potential coupling is considered, the well-known Diósi-Penrose collapse model acting on positional degrees of freedom is recovered. However, upon including the gravitomagnetic vector potential, additional collapse mechanisms emerge for rotational degrees of freedom as well as for mixed mass-rotation contributions.
summary: The paper extends the Diósi-Penrose model of gravitational collapse by incorporating the gravitomagnetic sector of linearized gravity. This extension predicts that rotation and mass currents induce new types of decoherence that are absent in standard mass-density-based models. The findings have significant implications for both laboratory-scale optomechanics and astrophysical observations of pulsars.
main_problem: The existing Diósi-Penrose (DP) model of gravitational collapse is limited to positional decoherence and fails to account for rotational degrees of freedom and mass currents.
main_result: The authors derive a post-Newtonian gravitational collapse model from linearized gravity that introduces new decoherence mechanisms for rotational and mixed mass-rotation degrees of freedom.
method: The study uses a hybrid classical-quantum dynamics approach based on gravitoelectromagnetism, employing the Aleksandrov bracket and Gaussian white noise to derive a generalized master equation.
model_or_system: A hybrid system where a quantum matter sector (mass density and currents) couples to a classical gravitational sector (gravitoelectric and gravitomagnetic potentials) within the weak-field limit of General Relativity.
key_observables: Decoherence rates for translational, rotational, and mixed channels; angular momentum expectation values; mass density distribution.
important_parameters: Post-Newtonian regime (non-negligible velocity); mass currents; gravitomagnetic field; mass density; surface velocity of rotating objects.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the weak-field, slow-motion (linearized gravity) approximation and a hybrid classical-quantum framework with Gaussian white noise.
figures_summary: Figure 1(a) illustrates a gedankenexperiment where a quantum system in a momentum eigenstate sources a gravitational field to show how a noiseless potential would violate the uncertainty principle; Figure 1(b) is incomplete in notes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the problem of rotational decoherence, derives a new master equation using linearized gravity and gravitoelectromagnetism, compares the results to the standard DP model, and concludes with experimental implications for optomechanics and pulsars.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it proposes a fundamental, gravity-induced decoherence mechanism that affects rotational and angular momentum degrees of freedom, which is critical for high-precision mesoscopic experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Sketch of a quantum system prepared in a momentum eigenstate with ˆ⃗p(t0). It sources a gravitational field which can be read out from the phase shift ∆φ ∝ R dt 1/|ˆ⃗x|. Since the phase can be obtained without performing a measurement on the system, the uncertainty relation (C2) is violated. (b) Sketch of a rotating quantum system enclosed by a ring interferometer. In the displayed setup, the interferometer plane corresponds to the x-y plane. The classical field ⃗A is sourced by ˆLz, thus leading to a phase shift ∆φ ∝ˆLz. If additional interferometers are placed at the x-z and y-z planes, all angular momentum components could be measured with arbitrarily high precision, which...
